coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission ratio fluorescence detection of bleomycin based on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots@gold nanoclusters assembly
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Dandan
Wang, Mengke
Liu, Qing
Chen, Junyang
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a dual-emission ratio fluorescence probe for copper ions (Cu2+)-based bleomycin (BLM) detection was established for the first time. The ratio fluorescence probe, nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots@gold nanoclusters (NGQDs@AuNCs), showed two well-resolved emission peaks at 440 nm and 580 nm respectively. Fluorescence intensity of AuNCs at 580 nm can be quenched by Cu2+ but restored in the presence of BLM, while fluorescence intensity of NGQDs at 440 nm remained unchanged throughout the process. The Cu2+-triggered fluorescence quenching can be attributed to aggregation-induced quenching mechanism, and the strong affinity between Cu2+ and BLM can make the quenched fluorescence turn on. Under optimal conditions, the BLM can be detected in the range of 0.8–1800 nM and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.27 nM. Furthermore, the established method in the application of human serum samples has been confirmed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratio fluorescence
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoclusters
Copper ions
Bleomycin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and purification of NGQDs, AuNCs and NGQDs@AuNCs
BLM assay in human serum samples
Characterization of NGQDs, AuNCs and NGQDs@AuNCs
Feasibility and mechanism for BLM assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of NGQDs; (B) TEM image of AuNCs. Inset showed high-resolution TEM; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of NGQDs and AuNCs, inset was photographs of NGQDs and AuNCs under UV light; (D) FT-IR spectra of GSH (line a), AuNCs (line b), NGQDs (line c) and NGQDs@AuNCs (line d); (E) Fluorescence spectra of NGQDs, AuNCs, NGQDs@AuNCs and mixture of NGQDs and AuNCs; (F) Fluorescence intensity of AuNCs to show concentration effect (gray column) and the FRET effect (blue column) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of NGQDs@AuNCs, NGQDs@AuNCs/BLM, NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+ and NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+/BLM; (B) RLS spectra of Cu2+, NGQDs@AuNCs and NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+; (C) TEM image of AuNCs aggregation in the presence of Cu2+; (D) Fluorescence lifetimes of NGQDs@AuNCs in the absence and presence of Cu2+.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+ under various concentrations of BLM, from bottom to top: 0, 0.8, 1.0, 8.0, 30.0, 60.0, 90.0, 130.0, 200.0, 500.0, 800.0, 1000.0, 1500.0, 1800.0, 2000.0 and 2200.0 nM, respectively; (B) The relationship between fluorescence intensity F580/F440 and the BLM concentration. Conditions: [Cu2+] = 3 μM, pH = 7.0.

(A) The selectivity of NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+ for BLM assay; (B) Fluorescence intensity ratio F580/F440 of NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+/BLM with the interfering substances. (1800 nM for BLM; 9000 nM for kanamycin, penicillin, chloromycetin, erythromycin, histidine, glutamate, alanine, L-phenylalanine, aspartic acid, glycine, L-threonine, arginine, threonine, ascorbic acid, dopamine; 1800 nM for vancomycin; 20 μM for glucose; 300 μM for Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+).

(A) Fluorescence spectra of NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+ system in the presence of different concentrations of BLM in human serum; (B) The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio F580/F440 and the BLM concentration in human serum. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three replicate measurements.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission ratio fluorescence detection of bleomycin based on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots@gold nanoclusters assembly

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of ratio fluorescence probe for bleomycin detection based on nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots@gold nanoclusters assembly.

Herein, a dual-emission ratio fluorescence probe for copper ions (Cu2+)-based bleomycin (BLM) detection was established for the first time. The ratio fluorescence probe, nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots@gold nanoclusters (NGQDs@AuNCs), showed two well-resolved emission peaks at 440 nm and 580 nm respectively. Fluorescence intensity of AuNCs at 580 nm can be quenched by Cu2+ but restored in the presence of BLM, while fluorescence intensity of NGQDs at 440 nm remained unchanged throughout the process. The Cu2+-triggered fluorescence quenching can be attributed to aggregation-induced quenching mechanism, and the strong affinity between Cu2+ and BLM can make the quenched fluorescence turn on. Under optimal conditions, the BLM can be detected in the range of 0.8–1800 nM and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.27 nM. Furthermore, the established method in the application of human serum samples has been confirmed.

ce:section.ce:para: Bleomycin (BLM) is an anti-tumor antibiotic separated from Streptomyces species, and it commonly used to treat some diseases such as squamous cell carcinoma [1]. Abuse of BLM will lead to adverse side-effect such as renal and pulmonary toxicity, notably interstitial pneumonitis and/or pulmonary fibrosis [2]. It is essential to detect BLM quantitatively in drug analysis and clinical research. Several methods have been reported such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], electrochemistry [4], electrogenerated chemiluminescence [5], colorimetry [6], fluorescence [7]. Among these methods, fluorescence analysis methods have drawn extensive attention owing to its facile operation, rapid response and high sensitivity. For example, Wang et al. developed a bicyclo-hairpin probe mediated strand displacement amplification strategy to detect BLM [8]. Yan and co-workers reported a “turn off” fluorescence method based on BLM/Fe2+ incising molecular beacon to make fluorescence of DNA-AgNCs reduced [9]. Nevertheless, most operation steps are more complicated and DNA sequences, especially modified ones, might lead to a high cost. Establishing an easy-operated, rapid and low-priced method for BLM assay is indispensable.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been used extensively by right of their unique characteristics of strong photoluminescence preferable biocompatibility, good water-solubility and low photobleaching [10]. Doping GQDs with nitrogen atoms can effectively adjust electronic characteristics, surface and local chemical features. The predominant luminescence property of NGQDs makes them to be applicable in the area of biomedical imaging, chem/biosensing and other optoelectronic applications [11,12]. Undersized metal nanoclusters (NCs) have excellent properties associated with physical and chemical characteristics [13]. Among these NCs, AuNCs have attracted growing interest by right of excellent photostability, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. Previous studies have been reported that AuNCs have been diffusely used to monitor metal ions [14], enzymes [15] and other biomolecules [16]. That AuNCs exhibit fluorescence emission in the near-infrared region makes it a significant advantage in biological detection.

Ratio fluorescence methods have sparked widespread interest, which determine the analyte based on the relationship between the ratio of two or more emission intensities and analyte concentration [17]. Compared with single-wavelength measurements, ratio fluorescence methods can provide built-in correction, which helps eliminate the limitations of the surrounding environment effectively and improve the reliability of detection results. The design principles of ratio fluorescence probes mainly include fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), chemical bond energy transfer (TBET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and excimer. It is usually constructed via the combination of organic fluorescence dyes, semiconductor quantum dots and fluorescence nanomaterials [18,19]. Li and co-workers established a ratio system based on GQDs and Eu3+ for selective detection of tetracyclines [20]. Yu et al. developed a ratio fluorescence probe through the electrostatic interaction of AuNCs and carbon dots for cysteine detection [21]. Lu et al. reported a ratio fluorescence strategy on bisphenol A assay on the basis of dual emission nanoparticles (D-NPs) [22]. In general, ratio fluorescence methods have been widely used due to their stable fluorescence performance, strong anti-interference ability and dual fluorescence emission characteristics.

Our group has reported a single-wavelength method for BLM detection based on BLM/Fe2+ incising 5’-AA-3’, 5’-AT-3’, 5’-GT-3’ and 5’-GC-3’ sites in ssDNA [23]. The current proposed dual-emission ratio fluorescence detection method effectively avoided the disadvantage that Fe2+ was easily oxidized, and reduced instrument errors compared with single-wavelength detection. In present work, an easy-operated, rapid and low-priced BLM detection method based on a ratio fluorescence probe was constructed for the first time. The detection strategy was illustrated in Scheme 1 . The ratio fluorescence probe was established via activated coupling reaction between NGQDs and AuNCs, and showed two well-resolved emission peaks under the same excitation. Fluorescence intensity of AuNCs at 580 nm can be quenched by Cu2+ due to Cu2+-induced AuNCs aggregation but restored in the presence of BLM due to the strong affinity between Cu2+ and BLM, while fluorescence intensity of NGQDs at 440 nm remained unchanged throughout the process. Furthermore, the detection method took less time compared with other BLM analysis methods and displayed high selectivity and anti-interference ability.
The reagents and apparatus were shown in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and purification of NGQDs, AuNCs and NGQDs@AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs were prepared referring to the literature [24]. 0.016 g of CA was added in 4.0 mL of ultrapure water (≥18 MΩ cm) and mixed with 2.0 mL NH3•H2O. The obtained mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 3 h. The obtained NGQDs were purified using 8–14 kDa dialysis bag for 16 h to remove impurities and stored at 4 °C. AuNCs were synthesized according to the reported method [25]. HAuCl4 (20 mM, 2.0 mL) and GSH (100 mM, 0.6 mL) were mixed with 17.4 mL water. The mixture was stirred in the dark and heated at 70 °C for 24 h. AuNCs were purified using 1000 Da dialysis bag for 16 h and then dried at 60 °C for the following experiment.

NGQDs@AuNCs were prepared via activated coupling reaction. Briefly, 5.0 mg EDC was added to 2.0 mL NGQDs (100 times diluted) and stirred for 10 min in the dark. Then 2.0 mL AuNCs solution (1.0 mg/mL) were added in the system and stirred at room temperature for another 2 h. Ultimately, the obtained NGQDs@AuNCs were stored at 4 °C.

For BLM detection, 30 μL Cu2+ (100 μM), 50 μL different concentrations of BLM (0–2200 nM), 500 μL PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.0) and 370 μL deionized water was equilibrated at 25 °C for 10 min. Afterwards, 50 μL NGQDs@AuNCs solution was added to the mixture. The fluorescence emission spectra were measured under the 320 nm excitation after 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: BLM assay in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum samples were obtained from the Changchun China-Japan Union Hospital. Acetonitrile was added to segregate the blood samples and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (6820 g) for 10 min to obtain human serum samples, the obtained supernatant was diluted ten times. Various concentrations of BLM were added in the sample before acetonitrile addition to prepare the spiked samples. For BLM assay in real sample, operation procedure was the same as the part 2.4 and replace BLM by serum sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of NGQDs, AuNCs and NGQDs@AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM images revealed that the average diameters of NGQDs and AuNCs were about 2.03 nm and 2.40 nm, respectively (Fig. 1 A and B and Fig. S1). The maximum fluorescence emission wavelengths of NGQDs and AuNCs were 440 nm and 580 nm, respectively (Fig. 1C). The inset displayed the photos of N-GQDs and AuNCs under UV light. The UV–vis spectrum of N-GQDs showed a peak at 305 nm and AuNCs showed a shoulder peak at around 410 nm. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of NGQDs and AuNCs were 26.7% and 3.4% using quinine sulfate (QY, 0.546 in 0.1 M H2SO4) and Rodamine 6 G (QY, 0.95 in ethanol) as reference material, respectively. Fig. 1D displayed the FT-IR spectra of GSH (line a), AuNCs (line b), NGQDs (line c) and NGQDs@AuNCs (line d), respectively. Compared with the FT-IR spectra of GSH and GSH-AuNCs, the characteristic peak for -SH (2520 cm−1) was not observed in the FT-IR spectrum of GSH-AuNCs, which indicated formation of GSH stabilized AuNCs through Au and -SH in GSH. The characteristic stretching vibration for CN (1455 cm−1), CO (1655 cm-1), NH (3307 cm−1 and 3189 cm−1) and the characteristic bending vibration for NH (1556 cm−1) in the FT-IR spectrum of NGQDs@AuNCs, indicating the formation of amide bond between NGQDs and AuNCs. Resonance light scattering (RLS) data showed that the self-condensation of NGQDs or AuNCs was far less than the covalent coupling between NGQDs and AuNCs (Fig. S2). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement (Fig. S3) showed that the peaks at 2θ of 22° and 32° in NGQDs@AuNCs matched with diffraction of carbon in NGQDs and diffraction of gold in AuNCs, respectively [26–28], which further confirm the formation of NGQDs@AuNCs.

Optimization conditions for NGQDs@AuNCs assembly were studied in the Supplementary Material and the optimized condition was pH 7.0 and reaction time of 2 h (Fig. S4). The fluorescence spectrum of NGQDs@AuNCs (Fig. 1E) displayed two well-resolved emission peaks under the excitation wavelength of 320 nm, and fluorescence intensity of NGQDs@AuNCs decreased to about 60% at 440 nm and increased to about 210% at 580 nm, while fluorescence intensity of NGQDs and AuNCs mixing solution without EDC activated coupling decreased to about 95% at 440 nm and increased to about 120% at 580 nm. Fluorescence excitation spectrum of AuNCs overlapped with the emission spectrum of NGQDs in 400–500 nm (Fig. S5). The results above can be attributed to the FRET effect between NGQDs and AuNCs, in which the fluorescence of NGQDs was reduced and the fluorescence of AuNCs was enhanced. Fluorescence intensity at 440 nm gradually decreased while that at 580 nm gradually increased with increasing concentrations of AuNCs (Fig. S6A).Taking into account concentration effect of AuNCs, a blank experiment was carried out without NGQDs. Fig. S6B displayed that fluorescence intensity of AuNCs enhanced as concentrations increased. Nevertheless, the extent of increase was significantly less than that of the NGQDs@AuNCs assembly, which confirmed the existence of FRET effect between NGQDs and AuNCs. All in all, the enhanced fluorescence of AuNCs was composed of concentration effect (gray column) and FRET effect (blue column), in which FRET effect played a significant role (Fig. 1F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility and mechanism for BLM assay
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of our method was explored. As exhibited in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence of NGQDs@AuNCs at 580 nm can be quenched by Cu2+, while that at 440 nm remained almost unchanged. After adding BLM, the fluorescence at 580 nm was restored to about 95% of the original. What more, addition had no significant effect on NGQDs@AuNCs. When adding the same amount of Cu2+, BLM and Cu2+/BLM to individual AuNCs or NGQDs, we found that Cu2+ and BLM can quench and restore the fluorescence of AuNCs respectively, but had no significant effect on the fluorescence of NGQDs (Fig. S7). The above results demonstrated that our method for BLM was feasible and it can provide an internal correction to reduce the instrument error.

The designed strategy for BLM detection was exhibited in Scheme 1. GSH is a tripeptide composed of glutamic acid, cysteine and glycine with an amino and two carboxyl groups, which has strong affinity for Cu2+ [29]. The fluorescence quenching mechanism can be attributed to aggregation-induced quenching mechanism, which meant that the interaction between GSH and Cu2+ led to aggregation of AuNCs through Cu2+-GSH chelation process [30,31]. Resonance light scattering (RLS), TEM and fluorescence lifetime were carried out to confirm the quenching mechanism. RLS characteristics were bound up with the volume and morphology. Fig. 2B showed RLS spectra of Cu2+, NGQDs@AuNCs and NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+. It can be seen that Cu2+-triggered aggregation led to a significant increase of RLS intensity [32–34]. Moreover, Cu2+-triggered AuNCs aggregation quenching was confirmed by TEM image (Fig. 2C). Compared with AuNCs without Cu2+ addition, that AuNCs exhibited obvious aggregation after Cu2+ addition was observed. To explore whether the quenching process was static or dynamic, fluorescence lifetimes of NGQDs@AuNCs and NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+ were further investigated (Fig. 2D). As shown in Table S1, fluorescence lifetimes of NGQDs@AuNCs and NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+ were 3.132 μs and 1.557 μs, respectively. The shorter fluorescence lifetimes of NGQDs@AuNCs were obtained with the addition of Cu2+, indicating that Cu2+-triggered quenching process was attributed to dynamic quenching [35]. Besides, NGQDs@AuNCs exhibited their specificity for Cu2+ compared with other metal ions (Zn2+, Na+, Mg2+, K+, Mn2+, Al3+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Fe2+) (Fig. S8C). Previous studies indicated that BLM has a high affinity towards Cu2+ and they can interact to form 1:1 Cu2+-BLM coordination complex [36]. Cu2+ can bind to N atoms from imidazole, the deprotonated amide of β-hydroxyhistidine, pyrimidine, the β-aminoalanine primary amine and the β-aminoalanine secondary amine in BLM [37,38]. Cu2+ can also interact with oxygen-containing functional groups in BLM [39]. To the best of our knowledge, Cu2+ can combined with the sulfur-containing structure [40,41] and we speculated that this property also contributed to the formation of Cu2+-BLM coordination complex. Fig. S9 showed UV–vis absorption spectra of Cu2+, BLM and Cu2+/BLM. BLM displayed an absorption peak at around 290 nm, and a new peak at 243 nm appeared after adding Cu2+, which confirmed the formation of Cu2+-BLM coordination complex. Therefore, the addition of BLM will prevent the Cu2+-triggered AuNCs aggregation. So this method can be effectively applied for the determination of BLM.

To improve the sensitivity of this method, the concentration of Cu2+, pH, reaction time and temperature were systematically studied for BLM detection. Fig. S8B showed fluorescence spectra of NGQDs@AuNCs with various concentrations of Cu2+. Fluorescence intensity ratio of F580/F440 gradually decreased as Cu2+ concentrations increased and 3.0 μM Cu2+ was sufficient to induce the quenching of fluorescence of AuNCs (Fig. S10A). The change of fluorescence intensity ratio F580/F440 of NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+ and NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+/BLM system were investigated in the pH range of 4.5–8.5 (Fig. S10B). Both the quenching effect caused by Cu2+ and the recovery effect caused by BLM reached the maximum at pH 7.0. Fluorescence intensity ratio F580/F440 under different concentrations of Cu2+ gradually reduced as time increased and reached equilibrium at 2 min (Fig. S8A). Fluorescence intensity ratio F580/F440 under various concentrations of BLM gradually increased within 10 min and then remained changeless as time increased. Therefore, the optimal reaction time was 10 min for BLM detection (Fig. S10C). Fluorescence intensity ratio F580/F440 was generally relatively stable over the temperature range of 5–55 °C and the optimum temperature was at 25 °C (Fig. S10D).

As exhibited in Fig. 3 A, fluorescence intensity at 580 nm increased while that at 440 nm stayed almost the same as BLM concentrations increased. Fig. 3B displayed that fluorescence intensity ratio F580/F440 and BLM concentration showed good linear relationship (R2 = 0.994) from 0.8 to 1800 nM, and linear regression equation was F580/F440 = 0.114 + 1.983 × 10−4 [BLM] (nM). The LOD of BLM was 0.27 nM (3σ). In addition, the method exhibited comparable liner range and LOD on BLM assay compared with other reported methods listed in Table S2. What deserves to be mentioned is that our method took less time (within 12 min), showing its significant advantage in BLM detection.

We further evaluated the selectivity of the method. Antibiotics (BLM, kanamycin, penicillin, chloromycetin, erythromycin, vancomycin), common inorganic ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+), amino acids (histidine, glutamate, alanine, L-phenylalanine, aspartic acid, glycine, L-threonine, arginine, threonine) and other biological molecules (glucose, ascorbic acid, dopamine) were respectively added to the system. No significant changes were observed in fluorescence intensity ratio F580/F440 of NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+ after introducing these substances except BLM (Fig. 4 A). Interference experiments were performed and the results were showed in Fig. 4B. Interfering substances had no significant impact on fluorescence intensity ratio F580/F440 of NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+/BLM. All the above results showed that this method had high selectivity and anti-interference ability.

For the purpose of confirming the validity of the developed method for the real sample analysis, BLM was detected by means of spiking it in human serum samples. Fig. 5 A showed the detection results of the ratio fluorescence method in the presence of different concentration of BLM in serum samples. It can be found that fluorescence intensity at 580 nm gradually increased with BLM concentration increasing from 0 to 2200 nM while fluorescence intensity at 440 nm remained about the same. And a good linear relationship from 3 nM to 1800 nM between the fluorescence intensity ratio F580/F440 and BLM concentration was displayed in Fig. 5B, the linear regression equation was F580/F440 = 0.113 + 1.879 × 10−4 [BLM] (nM) (R2 = 0.996), which showed a similar linear relationship compared to that obtained in pure reaction buffer, indicating that this method still manifested a sensitive response to BLM in serum samples. These results confirmed the reliability of NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+-based method for the detection of BLM in complex biological samples, and it can effectively illustrate the feasibility of proposed method in practical applications [42,43].

In summary, a simple, rapid and low-cost strategy was constructed for BLM detection based on the dual-emission ratio fluorescence NGQDs@AuNCs. NGQDs@AuNCs were obtained via activated coupling reaction and showed two well-resolved emission peaks at 440 nm and 580 nm respectively. What deserves to be mentioned is that NGQDs@AuNCs/Cu2+-based method took less time with high specificity and good anti-interference ability, showing its significant advantage in BLM detection. Moreover, it has been triumphantly used in determination of BLM in real samples and exhibited its potential application in chemical/biological analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775052and No.21575048), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No. 20180414013GH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.03.126.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dandan Su is currently carrying out her MS degree under the guidance of Professor Su in Jilin University. Her research is mainly focused on the mental nanoclusters and quantum dots and their applications for biosensors.

Mengke Wang is currently carrying out her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Professor Su in Jilin University. Her research is mainly focused on the mental nanoclusters and quantum dots and their applications for biosensors.

Qing Liu received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 2016. Now, she is currently carrying out her graduate work for her Doctor's degree under the guidance of Prof. Xingguang Su in Jilin University. Her research is mainly focused on the synthesis and functionalization of metal nanoclusters and their application on biological enzyme detection.

Junyang Chen is pursuing his PhD study under the guidance of Professor Xingguang Su in Jilin University (China). His research is mainly focused on the synthesis of silicon nanoparticles and their application on sensing.

Xingguang Su is a professor at the Department of Analytical Chemistry at the College of Chemistry, Jilin University. She received her MS degree from Jilin University (China) in 1992 and her PhD degree from Jilin University (China) in 1999. Her research focuses on the synthesis, characterization, functionalization and application of quantum dots and quantum dots-tagged microspheres in biomedicine.
